title: Silencing the self: schizophrenia as a self-disturbance
finding_one_line: Published Schizophrenia Bulletin first-person account describing psychosis as disintegration of the self and loss of ownership of one's own thoughts, emotions and actions (distinct from Chadwick 2007 / Gardiner 2021).
verbatim_quote: I was simply sectioned again, detached from my real self, observing what was being done to me in a third-person perspective. ... My thoughts, my emotions, and my actions, none of them belong to me any more.
claims_it_can_support: A real voice: what an acute psychotic episode feels like subjectively
Passivity / loss-of-self phenomena are a core lived experience

title: Night's end
finding_one_line: Second real Schizophrenia Bulletin first-person account (Roberta Payne) with quotable auditory-hallucination descriptions; backup FPA.
verbatim_quote: My brain created disc jockeys in the air vents of the hospital room playing Beatles' songs. ... I experienced extraordinarily loud fighter jets landing and taking off in my hospital room!
claims_it_can_support: First-person illustration of auditory hallucinations experienced as fully real, external perceptions

title: Failure of hypothesis evaluation as a factor in delusional belief
finding_one_line: Mechanistic definition source: a delusion is characterized by maintenance of a belief despite disconfirming evidence (two-factor theory 'second factor').

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: definition of the defining feature of a delusion (survives counter-evidence)
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: In the two-factor theory of delusional belief, the second factor consists of a failure to reject hypotheses in the face of disconfirmatory evidence.

claims_it_can_support: A delusion is a fixed belief that persists despite evidence to the contrary

title: Experiences of hearing voices: analysis of a novel phenomenological survey
finding_one_line: Largest phenomenological survey of voice-hearing (n=153): voices are perceptual, heterogeneous, and frequently NOT literally auditory. Sample mixed diagnoses incl. non-clinical, phenomenology, not schizophrenia-only prevalence.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: proportion reporting thought-like or mixed (not literally auditory) voices
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Less than half of the participants reported hearing literally auditory voices--70 (46%) individuals reported either thought-like or mixed experiences.

claims_it_can_support: 'Hearing voices' is heterogeneous; nearly half thought-like/mixed

title: The current conceptualization of negative symptoms in schizophrenia
finding_one_line: Consensus source defining five negative-symptom domains (blunted affect, alogia, anhedonia, asociality, avolition), their two-factor structure, and key role in functional outcome.

numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: a) five constructs should be considered as negative symptoms, i.e. blunted affect, alogia, anhedonia, asociality and avolition; ... c) the five constructs cluster in two factors, one including blunted affect and alogia and the other consisting of anhedonia, avolition and asociality.

claims_it_can_support: Negative symptoms = avolition, anhedonia, alogia, blunted affect, asociality
They play a key role in functional outcome

title: The global cognitive impairment in schizophrenia: consistent over decades and around the world
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis (100 studies): generalized cognitive impairment of ~1 SD (grand mean g=-1.03), largest in processing speed and episodic memory; stable across decades and geography.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: overall neurocognitive impairment vs healthy controls (~1 SD)
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: patients with schizophrenia scored significantly lower than controls across all cognitive tests and domains, grand mean effect size, g=-1.03.
Patients showed somewhat larger impairments in the domains of processing speed (g=-1.25) and episodic memory (g=-1.23).

claims_it_can_support: Neurocognitive deficit averages ~1 SD below controls
Processing speed and memory hit hardest

title: Neurocognitive deficit in schizophrenia: a quantitative review of the evidence
finding_one_line: Landmark meta-analysis (204 studies, 22 test variables): moderate-to-large deficits (d>.60) across every cognitive domain measured.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: effect sizes across all 22 neurocognitive test variables
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Moderate to large raw effect sizes (d > .60) were obtained for all 22 neurocognitive test variables, and none of the associated confidence intervals included zero.

title: Overlooking the obvious: a meta-analytic comparison of digit symbol coding tasks and other cognitive measures in schizophrenia
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis (37 studies, 1961 patients): the single largest cognitive impairment is processing-speed/coding, g=-1.57.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: effect size for digit symbol coding (processing-speed) deficit
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: The weighted mean effect for digit symbol coding was g = -1.57 (95% confidence interval, -1.66 to -1.48), significantly larger than effects for episodic memory, executive functioning, and working memory.

claims_it_can_support: Processing-speed deficit is the largest single neurocognitive impairment (~1.5 SD)

title: What are the functional consequences of neurocognitive deficits in schizophrenia?
finding_one_line: Landmark review: neurocognition (esp. verbal memory, vigilance) predicts real-world outcome, whereas positive symptoms did NOT predict outcome in any study reviewed, the load-bearing tension.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: positive symptoms do not predict functional outcome; cognition does
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: psychotic symptoms were not significantly associated with outcome measures in any of the studies reviewed.

claims_it_can_support: Positive symptoms poor predictors of functioning; cognition strongest predictor

title: Negative symptoms have greater impact on functioning than positive symptoms in schizophrenia: analysis of CATIE data
finding_one_line: CATIE analysis (n=1447): functioning was more strongly related to negative symptoms than any other domain, including positive, with honest caveat that overall variance explained was small.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: CATIE sample; negative symptoms most related to functioning
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Baseline functioning and change in functioning were more strongly related to PANSS negative factor than any of the other symptoms - though the amount of variance explained by symptom changes in general was small.

claims_it_can_support: Negative symptoms outweigh positive symptoms as predictors of functional outcome

title: Probability of Transition to Psychosis in Individuals at Clinical High Risk: An Updated Meta-analysis
finding_one_line: Updated meta-analysis: ~19% of clinical-high-risk individuals transition to psychosis by 2 years and ~25% by 3 years, the prodrome/at-risk state.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: cumulative transition-to-psychosis risk in CHR-P at 3 years
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: In this meta-analysis, 25% of individuals at CHR-P developed psychosis within 3 years.

claims_it_can_support: The clinical-high-risk/prodromal state carries substantial but not certain risk (~1 in 4 by 3 years)

title: Quantifying heterogeneity attributable to polythetic diagnostic criteria: theoretical framework and empirical application
finding_one_line: Combinatorial + empirical analysis: two people with the same diagnosis can share no symptoms; any two symptom combinations share on average less than half their symptoms.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: symptom overlap between two patients with the same diagnosis under polythetic criteria
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: in most categories, 2 individuals with the same diagnosis may share no symptoms in common, and that any 2 theoretically possible symptom combinations will share on average less than half their symptoms.

claims_it_can_support: The diagnosis is polythetic; two diagnosed people may share almost no symptoms

title: Prevalence and classification of hallucinations in multiple sensory modalities in schizophrenia spectrum disorders
finding_one_line: GROUP-cohort study: auditory hallucinations occur in 60-80% of patients, but hallucinations are frequently multimodal (53%) rather than auditory-only (27% unimodal).

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: proportion of schizophrenia-spectrum patients with auditory hallucinations
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Auditory hallucinations are experienced by 60-80% of all patients diagnosed with a schizophrenia spectrum disorder.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: hallucinations in multiple modalities vs single
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: 27% of the participants reported unimodal hallucinations and 53% MMHs.

claims_it_can_support: Auditory is most common (~60-80%) but hallucinations NOT always auditory

title: Lack of insight among outpatients with schizophrenia
finding_one_line: Outpatient study (n=87): ~half (49.5%) had at least moderately impaired insight and 25% severe deficits, insight is variably impaired along a spectrum, not simply absent.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: proportion of stable outpatients with at least moderately impaired insight
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: The illness insight of 43 subjects, or 49.5 percent, was at least moderately impaired.
Twenty-one subjects, or 25 percent, had severe insight deficits.

claims_it_can_support: Impaired insight is common but graded/variable, not all-or-nothing
